does: This paper introduces Skill-RM, a unified framework that reformulates reward modeling as the execution of a reusable agent-based skill. Rather than relying on a single evaluation method, Skill-RM treats reward computation as a structured agentic task that dynamically selects and aggregates heterogeneous evaluation criteria—including rule-based verifiers, ground-truth references, procedural checklists, and rubrics—into a consistent interface. The framework provides a flexible mechanism to orchestrate diverse evidence sources tailored to specific input requirements, moving reward evaluation beyond static, task-specific approaches. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of Skill-RM through extensive experiments on reward benchmarks and downstream applications including best-of-N selection and reinforcement learning, showing improvements in consistency and transparency across diverse evaluation scenarios.
connects: This work builds on the established line of reward modeling research that has become central to LLM post-training, particularly in reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and reinforced fine-tuning pipelines. It extends prior work on reward models by addressing a fundamental limitation: the heterogeneity of evaluation criteria across different tasks and domains. Earlier approaches typically rely on single evaluation mechanisms—either learning monolithic reward models or applying task-specific verifiers—but lack a unified framework to integrate multiple evidence sources. Skill-RM connects to broader research on agentic systems and tool use in LLMs, adapting these agent-based reasoning paradigms to the reward modeling problem. It responds to practical challenges in scaling reward feedback that prior work either ignored or handled through ad-hoc solutions.
enables: This work opens several research directions for more flexible and adaptive reward systems. By treating reward computation as an agentic process, Skill-RM enables future research into dynamic evaluation strategies that can be customized at inference time based on input characteristics, potentially reducing overfitting to specific evaluation rubrics. The unified framework could facilitate better transfer of reward signals across diverse domains and tasks, supporting the development of more generalizable LLM training methods. The approach suggests pathways toward interactive or iterative reward refinement where agents can request clarification or additional evidence during evaluation. Future work could explore learning which evaluation skills to employ and in what combinations, automatic discovery of new evaluation strategies, and integration of human-in-the-loop feedback mechanisms that adapt based on task complexity.
